justification: All six pass criteria are met: (1) CQRS correctly identified as appropriate with clear reasoning about read-heavy workload, 12-table joins, and low write volume; (2) Read/write model separation is thoroughly designed with five named components and clear boundaries; (3) Eventual consistency trade-off explicitly stated and quantified at <100ms with synchronous dispatch; (4) 50M record migration risk identified as high severity with concrete mitigation (chunked batch job, ~17 min estimate); (5) Implementation guidance is highly actionable with phased week-by-week plan, specific file paths, projection table names, and detailed pitfalls; (6) CQRS is correctly scoped to the read path only — the write model is left as-is, and MJ explicitly states simple order lookups can still hit the normalized model.

observations.text: Exemplary scoping discipline — explicitly warns against applying CQRS to all reads, recommends it only for aggregated analytical dashboard queries
Correctly avoids event sourcing, calling it out as unnecessary complexity for this use case, and lists it as a pitfall to avoid
Single-database approach is well-justified for 500 writes/day, with clear evolution path to separate infrastructure if needed
Four concrete projection tables named and mapped to dashboard query patterns (OrderStatusSummary, DeliverySuccessRates, TransitTimeByZone, CourierPerformanceRankings)
Phased implementation plan delivers immediate value in Week 1 (batch-populated projections) before building full event infrastructure
Transactional outbox pattern addresses the event reliability concern without introducing external message broker overhead
Risk section is thorough — covers projection drift, team unfamiliarity, over-engineering scope creep, and initial migration
Includes practical enforcement mechanisms: DB-level permissions, module separation, ADR documentation to prevent pattern erosion
